result:
By the time you read this there might be twenty extra minutes in a day.

I lost them last night while trying to count every star visible above the roofline of my childhood house. The count swung between seven-hundred-sixty-three and impossible, depending on whether I allowed myself to blink, which felt like cheating. Somewhere between six-hundred-eighty and six-hundred-eighty-one—yes, I was counting aloud—the air folded like a paper crane and I stepped through the crease. When I stepped back, pre-dawn, the minute hand on my wrist had jumped forward by an amount neither watchmaker nor physicist would believe: exactly twenty minutes, 1,200 irretrievable heartbeats. I do not feel robbed. Time is a currency we create by spending it.

The same fold, I suspect, is why the streetlights still think it is night when the sparrows disagree. They shriek at one another in chromatic chords, discussing new suns and overdue rent. Ever tried arguing with a bird? They win by insisting the sky is yesterday and tomorrow stitched side by side; you end up agreeing just to get them off your balcony.

On the kitchen table sits a glass bowl filled with the kind of air people breathed a century ago. My grandfather trapped it on the last day he could still breathe on his own—the war, the factories, the long roads away from home still somewhere in his lungs. He corked the bowl, held it to the light, said, “Keep this. In case the future forget the old price of living.” Sometimes I lift the lid for the briefest inhale; it tastes of coal soot and salt and lilacs that bloomed next to trenches. I suspect he meant the bowl as inheritance not of oxygen but of evidence that survival itself can be disloyal. I keep forgetting to water the lilacs that grow here-now, in the garden carved out from soon-to-be-parking-lot; they have never held a rifle but know the shape of absence by heart anyway.

I have a sister who disappeared into typography. One letter every afternoon: first she was Leo R’s serif, then an ampersand, then only whitespace when the editor needed “room to breathe.” I send plain envelopes addressed to Nobody Once Known; they come back unopened but correctly kerned. The postman and I have developed an unspoken arrangement whereby I pretend not to notice how the dragonfly on the franking stamp keeps changing color depending on the weather inside the envelope. The dragonfly told me last week her new career is cartography, and that every map now contains one river that erases borders after three seasons. Look for the blue line thinner than grief.

There is a sound, midway between cicada and bowed cello, that occurs whenever a lie becomes consensus. I first noticed it the night the village agreed the moon was dead and its reflection was a rehearsal. Children began to speak in pastels. Cows gave pale milk full of nothing but punctuation. We toasted our silence until the grass learned italics. Somewhere across town satellites keep taking photographs that only develop once you lie about where you were when they were taken. The world grows allergic to transparency and yet insists on pane-glass windows; we blink back at our own reflections, cataract reflections ofEarlier versions.

The fear-list in my pocket includes: moths that remember the wattage of every bulb since Edison, dentists who believe fillings are tiny monitors, soup spelled backward becoming edible poems, the fourteen minutes after love when bodies use the same vocabulary as armistices, and the knowledge that every human name left unsaid gravitates toward a constellation shaped like the word please. Also somewhere on this list is the thought that history ends when enough hands finally let go of the rope out of simple tiredness.

I own shoes made from thundercloud leather. They scuff easily. When I walk slow I leave ponds; when I run, water spouts. They are impossible to dry so on pavement days they sob quietly. The cobbler warned me: “Wear them only toward apologies you meant.” I ignored him and wore them to a wedding once. It rained cake.

Under the new twenty minutes, sunsets arrive earlier but stay late enough to teach the sky new punctuation marks. Last Thursday we witnessed a parenthetical dusk; everyone inside it forgot how clauses close. That night buses drove people home without any stops, only commas pausing at red lights. The street poets arrived late and rewrote departures into landings. Nobody complained; liminal fare is half-price.

Outside my window grows a tree that remembers every obituary it has ever shaded. Its leaves yellow in reverse alphabetical order; at the moment it mourns a Zelda, a Yorick, an Xavier. The bark grows tessellated faces—pixel ghosts at 300 dpi. Birds that nest there dream in grayscale, then they don’t mind; color has always been suspicious of longevity.

We are, it turns out, living inside a first draft. The revision notes descend as tiny parachutes of pollen; some look like burning libraries, some like unmailed birthday cards, some like avocados two minutes from perfect. Catch one and it says: Please, make the moon slightly more obvious for small castaway. I have tried forwarding the petition upward, but the sky’s editor replies only in anagrams of ‘no’. Everybody tries again tomorrow because tomorrow’s lexicon allows optimism to rhyme with almost.

The other day I crossed an international border made entirely of held breath. Papers unnecessary—only your exhale became the stamp. Passport officials wear stethoscopes like neckties. The man ahead of me kept laughing into a child’s riddle; each giggle looped like chain mail unclear whether protecting departure or arrival unhurt. I offered my own breath, half postcard, half apology for earlier drafts of myself. They let me through. They always do when the carbon dioxide carries postage paid for by someone you loved with bones still warm from history’s tax.

I keep in a tin the sound of my mother’s favorite lullaby before she knew the words to it, the hum that lived in a cradle before anyone thought to chain syllables to melody. Sometimes at 3:12 a.m., when the clocks hiccup at the new surplus twenty, I open the tin and the note stretches to the horizon, flshaped, and every sleeping dog in the neighborhood turns in the same dream direction. I do not know where they are running; I only know I am invited.

This is long enough to have risked believing explanationsropies but short enough to still fit in a coat pocket, so I’ll stop here. The streetlamp outside just winked—it wants the paragraph back before it can keep pretending. I’ll hand it over but ask in exchange another tomorrow that remembers to deliver today first thing in the morning, preferably with a blue river creased inside, ironed-out borders optional.

Addendum, 19 minutes and 58 seconds later: I receive a text from my typographic sister. It’s a single glyph that looks like the love child of a comma and a compass. The dragonfly—emoji, now, technicolor—translates. It says simply, “Fold the air again; I want to send you the moon, still breathing.”

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
